html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Nine — Part Six

The Book of Tehillim

TEHILLIM

The Psalms — The Praises of Yisra'EL

Psalms 107 through 118

Book V Opening — The Egyptian Hallel — Psalms 113 Through 118

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

11Q5 (11QPsa) — Cave 11 — Major Psalms Scroll — primary source for Book V

4Q83 (4QPsa) — Cave 4 — Psalms 107-109

4Q84 (4QPsb) — Cave 4 — Psalms 91-118

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon — All 150 Psalms plus Psalm 151

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

BOOK V — PSALMS 107 THROUGH 118

TEHILLIM — PSALM 107 Oh Give Thanks to YHWH — He Gathered the Redeemed From Every Direction — Book V Opens

1 Oh give thanks to YHWH, for he is good, for his steadfast love endures forever.

2 Let the redeemed of YHWH say so, whom he has redeemed from trouble

3 And gathered in from the lands, from the east and from the west, from the north and from the south.

4 Some wandered in desert wastes, finding no way to a city to dwell in.

5 Hungry and thirsty, their soul fainted within them.

6 Then they cried to YHWH in their trouble, and he delivered them from their distress.

7 He led them by a straight way till they reached a city to dwell in.

8 Let them thank YHWH for his steadfast love, for his wondrous works to the children of man.

9 For he satisfies the longing soul, and the hungry soul he fills with good things.

10 Some sat in darkness and in the shadow of death, prisoners in affliction and in irons,

11 For they had rebelled against the words of Creator and spurned the counsel of the Most High.

12 So he bowed their hearts down with hard labor — they fell down, with none to help.

13 Then they cried to YHWH in their trouble, and he delivered them from their distress.

14 He brought them out of darkness and the shadow of death, and burst their bonds apart.

15 Let them thank YHWH for his steadfast love, for his wondrous works to the children of man.

16 For he shatters the doors of bronze and cuts in two the bars of iron.

17 Some were fools through their sinful ways, and because of their iniquities suffered affliction.

18 They loathed any kind of food, and they drew near to the gates of death.

19 Then they cried to YHWH in their trouble, and he delivered them from their distress.

20 He sent out his word and healed them, and delivered them from their destruction.

21 Let them thank YHWH for his steadfast love, for his wondrous works to the children of man.

22 And let them offer sacrifices of thanksgiving, and tell of his deeds in songs of joy.

23 Some went down to the sea in ships, doing business on the great waters.

24 They saw the deeds of YHWH, his wondrous works in the deep.

25 For he commanded and raised the stormy wind, which lifted up the waves of the sea.

26 They mounted up to heaven — they went down to the depths — their courage melted away in their evil plight.

27 They reeled and staggered like drunken men and were at their wits' end.

28 Then they cried to YHWH in their trouble, and he delivered them from their distress.

29 He made the storm be still, and the waves of the sea were hushed.

30 Then they were glad that the waters were quiet, and he brought them to their desired haven.

31 Let them thank YHWH for his steadfast love, for his wondrous works to the children of man.

32 Let them extol him in the congregation of the people, and praise him in the assembly of the elders.

33 He turns rivers into a desert, springs of water into thirsty ground,

34 A fruitful land into a salty waste, because of the evil of its inhabitants.

35 He turns a desert into pools of water, a parched land into springs of water.

36 And there he lets the hungry dwell, and they establish a city to live in.

37 They sow fields and plant vineyards and get a fruitful yield.

38 By his blessing they multiply greatly, and he does not let their livestock diminish.

39 When they are diminished and brought low through oppression, evil, and sorrow,

40 He pours contempt on princes and makes them wander in trackless wastes.

41 But he raises up the needy out of affliction and makes their families like flocks.

42 The upright see it and are glad, and all wickedness shuts its mouth.

43 Whoever is wise, let him attend to these things — let them consider the steadfast love of YHWH.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 107 opens Book V of the Tehillim and is attested in 4QPsf (4Q88) from Cave 4. The psalm is structured around four refrains — then they cried to YHWH in their trouble and he delivered them — describing four types of human distress and YHWH's deliverance from each. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 107 in full. The fourfold pattern of distress, cry, deliverance, and thanksgiving is read as the universal covenant pattern applicable to every generation of YHWH's people.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 107. The Cave 4 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Psalm 107 opens Book V with a catalog of four types of human extremity: the desert wanderer — verses 4-9 — the prisoner — verses 10-16 — the sick fool — verses 17-22 — and the storm-tossed sailor — verses 23-32. Each ends with the same refrain: let them thank YHWH for his steadfast love, for his wondrous works to the children of man. The covenant pattern is consistent across every category of suffering: cry to YHWH, receive deliverance, give thanks. Verse 43 — whoever is wise, let him attend to these things — let them consider the steadfast love of YHWH. Wisdom is the recognition of YHWH's steadfast love across all circumstances.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 108 A Song — A Psalm of Dawid — My Heart Is Steadfast — Give Us Help Against the Foe

1 My heart is steadfast, O Creator — I will sing and make melody with all my being.

2 Awake, O harp and lyre — I will awake the dawn.

3 I will give thanks to you, O YHWH, among the peoples — I will sing praises to you among the nations.

4 For your steadfast love is great above the heavens — your faithfulness reaches to the clouds.

5 Be exalted, O Creator, above the heavens — let your glory be over all the earth.

6 That your beloved ones may be delivered, give salvation by your right hand and answer me.

7 Creator has spoken in his holiness: With exultation I will divide up Shekhem and portion out the Vale of Sukkot.

8 Gile'ad is mine — Menasheh is mine — Efrayim is my helmet — Yehudah is my scepter.

9 Moav is my washbasin — upon Edom I cast my shoe — over Peleshet I shout in triumph.

10 Who will bring me to the fortified city? Who will lead me to Edom?

11 Have you not rejected us, O Creator? You do not go out, O Creator, with our armies.

12 Oh grant us help against the foe, for vain is the salvation of man.

13 With Creator we shall do valiantly — it is he who will tread down our foes.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 108 is a composite psalm, combining Psalm 57:7-11 and Psalm 60:5-12 into a single new composition. It is attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. The deliberate combination of two earlier psalms into one confirms the ancient editorial practice of reusing and recontextualizing covenant poetry.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 108 in full. The psalm is read as a prayer of covenant confidence — the steadfast heart of verse 1 meeting the geographical survey of the covenant land in verses 7-9.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 108. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1 — my heart is steadfast, O Creator — I will sing and make melody with all my being. The same declaration of steadfastness opens Psalm 57:7 — the source text for this opening section. The steadfast heart is the covenant posture that makes praise possible regardless of circumstances. Verse 13 — with Creator we shall do valiantly — it is he who will tread down our foes. The identical closing of Psalm 60:12. The reuse of these verses in a new composition confirms that the covenant community treated their own scriptures as living material to be drawn on, combined, and reapplied to new situations.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 109 Of Dawid — A Psalm — O Creator of My Praise, Do Not Be Silent — The Imprecatory Psalm

1 O Creator of my praise, do not be silent.

2 For wicked and deceitful mouths are opened against me, speaking against me with lying tongues.

3 They encircle me with words of hate, and attack me without cause.

4 In return for my love they accuse me, but I give myself to prayer.

5 So they reward me evil for good, and hatred for my love.

6 Appoint a wicked man against him — let an accuser stand at his right hand.

7 When he is tried, let him come forth guilty — let his prayer be counted as sin.

8 May his days be few — may another take his office.

9 May his children be fatherless and his wife a widow.

10 May his children wander about and beg, seeking food far from the ruins they inhabit.

11 May the creditor seize all that he has — may strangers plunder the fruits of his toil.

12 Let there be none to extend kindness to him, nor anyone to pity his fatherless children.

13 May his posterity be cut off — may his name be blotted out in the second generation.

14 May the iniquity of his fathers be remembered before YHWH, and let not the sin of his mother be blotted out.

15 Let them be before YHWH continually, that he may cut off the memory of them from the earth.

16 For he did not remember to show kindness, but pursued the poor and needy and the brokenhearted, to put them to death.

17 He loved to curse — let curses come upon him. He did not delight in blessing — may it be far from him.

18 He clothed himself with cursing as his coat — may it soak into his body like water, like oil into his bones.

19 May it be like a garment that he wraps around him, like a belt that he puts on every day.

20 May this be the reward of my accusers from YHWH, of those who speak evil against my life.

21 But you, O YHWH my Sovereign, deal on my behalf for your name's sake — because your steadfast love is good, deliver me.

22 For I am poor and needy, and my heart is stricken within me.

23 I am gone like a shadow at evening — I am shaken off like a locust.

24 My knees are weak through fasting — my body has become gaunt, with no fat.

25 I am an object of scorn to my accusers — when they see me, they wag their heads.

26 Help me, O YHWH my Creator — save me according to your steadfast love.

27 Let them know that this is your hand — you, O YHWH, have done it.

28 Let them curse, but you will bless — they arise and are put to shame, but your servant will be glad.

29 My accusers will be clothed with dishonor — they will be wrapped in their own shame as in a cloak.

30 With my mouth I will give great thanks to YHWH — I will praise him in the midst of the throng.

31 For he stands at the right hand of the needy one, to save him from those who condemn his soul.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 109 is attested in 4QPsf (4Q88) from Cave 4. The psalm is one of the most extended imprecatory psalms in the Tehillim — a sustained calling for divine judgment against the psalmist's enemies. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 109 in full. The imprecatory sections — verses 6-20 — are read in the Beta Israel tradition as covenant curses invoked against those who have actively pursued the death of the innocent.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 109. The Cave 4 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 4 — in return for my love they accuse me, but I give myself to prayer. The Hebrew literally reads: but I am prayer — the psalmist has become the act of prayer itself. He has no other weapon. Verses 6-20 are covenant curses — the formal invocation of the covenant curse against covenant breakers, grounded in the curse formulae of Devarim 27-28. The psalmist does not take vengeance himself — he places the matter entirely in YHWH's hands. Verse 31 — he stands at the right hand of the needy one, to save him from those who condemn his soul. YHWH himself stands at the right hand of the poor — the advocate position — against those who condemn them.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 110 Of Dawid — A Psalm — YHWH Said to My Sovereign — You Are a Priest Forever

1 YHWH says to my Sovereign: Sit at my right hand, until I make your enemies your footstool.

2 YHWH sends forth from Tziyon your mighty scepter. Rule in the midst of your enemies.

3 Your people will offer themselves freely on the day of your power, in holy garments — from the womb of the morning, the dew of your youth will be yours.

4 YHWH has sworn and will not change his mind, You are a priest forever after the order of Melki-Tzedek.

5 YHWH is at your right hand — he will shatter kings on the day of his wrath.

6 He will execute judgment among the nations, filling them with corpses — he will shatter chiefs over the wide earth.

7 He will drink from the brook by the way — therefore he will lift up his head.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 110 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm is a royal oracle — YHWH speaking to the Davidic king. Verse 4 — you are a priest forever after the order of Melki-Tzedek — is the only explicit reference to the Melki-Tzedek priesthood in the canonical Tehillim, though Melki-Tzedek appears in Bereshit 14 and in the Qumran document 11QMelchizedek (11Q13). The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 110 in full. The Melki-Tzedek priesthood — verse 4 — is read in the Beta Israel tradition as the ancient pre-Levitical priestly order of Shalem identified in Bereshit 14:18-20.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 110. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading. The Qumran document 11QMelchizedek (11Q13) develops the Melki-Tzedek theology of Psalm 110 extensively, presenting Melki-Tzedek as a heavenly figure who executes YHWH's judgment at the end of days.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 4 — you are a priest forever after the order of Melki-Tzedek. This is the only covenant that combines royal and priestly function in a single figure in the canonical Tehillim. The Qumran document 11Q13 expands Melki-Tzedek into an eschatological figure who proclaims release at the end of the tenth Jubilee — drawing directly on Vayikra 25 and Yesha'yahu 61. The Melki-Tzedek of Psalm 110 and the Melki-Tzedek of 11Q13 together represent one of the most developed non-Masoretic theological trajectories in the entire DSS corpus.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 111 Praise YHWH — I Will Give Thanks to YHWH With My Whole Heart — An Acrostic

1 Praise YHWH. I will give thanks to YHWH with my whole heart, in the company of the upright, in the congregation.

2 Great are the works of YHWH, studied by all who delight in them.

3 Full of splendor and majesty is his work, and his righteousness endures forever.

4 He has caused his wondrous works to be remembered — YHWH is gracious and merciful.

5 He provides food for those who fear him — he remembers his covenant forever.

6 He has shown his people the power of his works, in giving them the inheritance of the nations.

7 The works of his hands are faithful and just — all his precepts are trustworthy.

8 They are established forever and ever, to be performed with faithfulness and uprightness.

9 He sent redemption to his people — he has commanded his covenant forever. Holy and awesome is his name.

10 The fear of YHWH is the beginning of wisdom — all those who practice it have a good understanding. His praise endures forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 111 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm is an alphabetic acrostic — each half-verse beginning with a successive letter of the Hebrew aleph-bet. Psalms 111 and 112 form a matched pair — 111 praising YHWH's works and 112 describing the blessed man who fears YHWH. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 111 in full. Verse 10 — the fear of YHWH is the beginning of wisdom — is one of the foundational covenant declarations in the Beta Israel tradition, connecting the Tehillim to the wisdom tradition of Mishlei 1:7.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 111. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 5 — he provides food for those who fear him — he remembers his covenant forever. The two halves of the verse are inseparable: provision and covenant memory. YHWH feeds those who fear him because he remembers the covenant in which he committed to provide. Verse 10 — the fear of YHWH is the beginning of wisdom. The same declaration that opens the Book of Mishlei — Proverbs 1:7. The Tehillim and the wisdom literature share the same foundation: covenant reverence before YHWH is not the conclusion of wisdom but its starting point.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 112 Praise YHWH — Blessed Is the Man Who Fears YHWH — An Acrostic

1 Praise YHWH. Blessed is the man who fears YHWH, who greatly delights in his commandments.

2 His offspring will be mighty in the land — the generation of the upright will be blessed.

3 Wealth and riches are in his house, and his righteousness endures forever.

4 Light dawns in the darkness for the upright — he is gracious, merciful, and righteous.

5 It is well with the man who deals generously and lends — who conducts his affairs with justice.

6 For the righteous will never be moved — he will be remembered forever.

7 He is not afraid of bad news — his heart is firm, trusting in YHWH.

8 His heart is steady — he will not be afraid — until he looks in triumph on his adversaries.

9 He has distributed freely — he has given to the poor — his righteousness endures forever — his horn is exalted in honor.

10 The wicked man sees it and is angry — he gnashes his teeth and melts away — the desire of the wicked will perish.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 112 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. It is the companion acrostic to Psalm 111 — where 111 praises YHWH's attributes, 112 describes the human being who embodies those same attributes through covenant faithfulness. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 112 in full. The blessed man of Psalm 112 is read as the practical outworking of Psalm 111's theology — the one who fears YHWH and delights in his commandments becomes the living image of YHWH's own character.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 112. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Psalms 111 and 112 form a theological diptych. In Psalm 111: YHWH is gracious and merciful — verse 4. In Psalm 112: the righteous man is gracious, merciful, and righteous — verse 4. In Psalm 111: his righteousness endures forever — verse 3. In Psalm 112: his righteousness endures forever — verse 3 and verse 9. The covenant person is called to embody the character of YHWH. The blessed man of Psalm 112 is not merely blessed by YHWH — he has become a mirror of YHWH's own steadfast love in his community.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 113 Praise YHWH — Who Is Like YHWH Our Creator — He Raises the Poor From the Dust

1 Praise YHWH. Praise, O servants of YHWH, praise the name of YHWH.

2 Blessed be the name of YHWH from this time forth and forevermore.

3 From the rising of the sun to its setting, the name of YHWH is to be praised.

4 YHWH is high above all nations, and his glory above the heavens.

5 Who is like YHWH our Creator, who is seated on high,

6 Who looks far down on the heavens and the earth?

7 He raises the poor from the dust and lifts the needy from the ash heap,

8 To make them sit with princes, with the princes of his people.

9 He gives the barren woman a home, making her the joyous mother of children. Praise YHWH.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 113 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm opens the Egyptian Hallel — Psalms 113-118 — the collection of praise psalms sung at the Pesach meal and at the major pilgrimage festivals. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 113 in full. The Egyptian Hallel psalms are central to the Beta Israel Pesach liturgy — sung in the context of the covenant memorial of the Exodus from Egypt.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 113. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verses 7-8 — he raises the poor from the dust and lifts the needy from the ash heap, to make them sit with princes. This is the covenant reversal theology of the Tehillim at its most concentrated. The ash heap is the place of the utterly destitute — Iyov 2:8. YHWH reaches into the lowest place of human experience and raises the person from there to sit with princes. Verse 9 — he gives the barren woman a home, making her the joyous mother of children. The barren woman made a mother echoes Shemu'el Aleph 2:5 — the Song of Khannah — and Bereshit 21 — the birth of Yitzhak to Sarah. YHWH reverses what appears irreversible.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 114 When Yisra'EL Went Out From Egypt — The Sea Fled — The Mountains Skipped

1 When Yisra'EL went out from Egypt, the house of Yaakov from a people of strange language,

2 Yehudah became his sanctuary, Yisra'EL his dominion.

3 The sea looked and fled — the Yarden turned back.

4 The mountains skipped like rams, the hills like lambs.

5 What ails you, O sea, that you flee? O Yarden, that you turn back?

6 O mountains, that you skip like rams? O hills, like lambs?

7 Tremble, O earth, at the presence of YHWH, at the presence of the Creator of Yaakov,

8 Who turns the rock into a pool of water, the flint into a spring of water.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 114 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm is a concentrated celebration of the Exodus — eight verses in which the entire cosmos responds to YHWH's presence with Yisra'EL. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 114 in full. The psalm is the second of the Egyptian Hallel and is sung at the Pesach as the covenant community's compact retelling of the foundational redemption.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 114. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3-4 — the sea looked and fled — the Yarden turned back — the mountains skipped like rams. Creation itself reacts to the presence of YHWH with Yisra'EL. The sea does not merely part — it flees. The Yarden does not merely stop — it turns back. The mountains do not merely stand still — they skip like rams. The playful, almost comic energy of these verses contains a profound theology: the entire natural order recognizes YHWH's presence and responds in awe. Verses 5-6 address the creation elements directly as if questioning them. Verse 7 answers: tremble, O earth, at the presence of YHWH — the Creator of Yaakov.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 115 Not to Us, O YHWH, Not to Us, But to Your Name Give Glory — Their Idols Are Silver and Gold

1 Not to us, O YHWH, not to us, but to your name give glory, for the sake of your steadfast love and your faithfulness.

2 Why should the nations say, Where is their Creator?

3 Our Creator is in the heavens — he does all that he pleases.

4 Their idols are silver and gold, the work of human hands.

5 They have mouths, but do not speak — eyes, but do not see.

6 They have ears, but do not hear — noses, but do not smell.

7 They have hands, but do not feel — feet, but do not walk — and they do not make a sound in their throat.

8 Those who make them become like them — so do all who trust in them.

9 O Yisra'EL, trust in YHWH — he is their help and their shield.

10 O house of Aharon, trust in YHWH — he is their help and their shield.

11 You who fear YHWH, trust in YHWH — he is their help and their shield.

12 YHWH has remembered us — he will bless us — he will bless the house of Yisra'EL — he will bless the house of Aharon.

13 He will bless those who fear YHWH, both the small and the great.

14 May YHWH give you increase, you and your children.

15 May you be blessed by YHWH, who made heaven and earth.

16 The heavens are YHWH's heavens, but the earth he has given to the children of man.

17 The dead do not praise YHWH, nor do any who go down into silence.

18 But we will bless YHWH from this time forth and forevermore. Praise YHWH.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 115 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm is a covenant anti-idolatry declaration — the contrast between YHWH who does all that he pleases and idols that cannot speak, see, hear, or move. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 115 in full. The threefold call to trust — Yisra'EL, the house of Aharon, those who fear YHWH — is read as the complete covenant community: the people, the priesthood, and all who have joined themselves to YHWH.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 115. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 8 — those who make them become like them — so do all who trust in them. The idol-maker becomes like his idol: incapable of genuine speech, sight, hearing. The covenant person is formed by what they worship. Those who trust in the living YHWH become alive in him — they speak, see, hear, respond. Those who trust in mute and blind idols become mute and blind. Verse 16 — the heavens are YHWH's heavens, but the earth he has given to the children of man. The earth is the domain entrusted to humanity — the same stewardship mandate of Bereshit 1:28. YHWH's gift of the earth to humanity is his covenant act of delegation.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 116 I Love YHWH Because He Has Heard My Voice — I Will Walk Before YHWH in the Land of the Living

1 I love YHWH, because he has heard my voice and my pleas for mercy.

2 Because he inclined his ear to me, therefore I will call on him as long as I live.

3 The snares of death encompassed me — the pangs of Sheol laid hold on me — I suffered distress and anguish.

4 Then I called on the name of YHWH: O YHWH, I pray, deliver my soul.

5 Gracious is YHWH, and righteous — our Creator is merciful.

6 YHWH preserves the simple — when I was brought low, he saved me.

7 Return, O my soul, to your rest, for YHWH has dealt bountifully with you.

8 For you have delivered my soul from death, my eyes from tears, my feet from stumbling.

9 I will walk before YHWH in the land of the living.

10 I believed, even when I spoke: I am greatly afflicted.

11 I said in my alarm, All mankind are liars.

12 What shall I render to YHWH for all his benefits to me?

13 I will lift up the cup of salvation and call on the name of YHWH.

14 I will pay my vows to YHWH in the presence of all his people.

15 Precious in the sight of YHWH is the death of his saints.

16 O YHWH, I am your servant — I am your servant, the son of your maidservant — you have loosed my bonds.

17 I will offer to you the sacrifice of thanksgiving and call on the name of YHWH.

18 I will pay my vows to YHWH in the presence of all his people,

19 In the courts of the house of YHWH, in your midst, O Yerushalaym. Praise YHWH.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 116 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm is a personal thanksgiving for deliverance from near-death — the psalmist fulfilling the vows made in distress by bringing the sacrifice of thanksgiving to the sanctuary. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 116 in full. Verse 15 — precious in the sight of YHWH is the death of his saints — is read in the Beta Israel tradition as the declaration that YHWH does not take the death of his covenant people lightly.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 116. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 15 — precious in the sight of YHWH is the death of his saints. The Hebrew yaqar — precious, costly, of great worth — is used of the most valuable things. The death of YHWH's saints is not cheap, not casual, not unnoticed. It costs something in YHWH's reckoning. Verse 9 — I will walk before YHWH in the land of the living. The response to deliverance from near-death is not relief but covenant orientation: the life given back will be lived before YHWH. Walking before YHWH is the same covenant posture as Avraham — Bereshit 17:1 — and Noakh — Bereshit 6:9.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 117 Praise YHWH, All Nations — His Steadfast Love Endures Forever — The Shortest Psalm

1 Praise YHWH, all nations — extol him, all peoples.

2 For great is his steadfast love toward us, and the faithfulness of YHWH endures forever. Praise YHWH.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 117 is the shortest psalm in the Tehillim and the shortest chapter in the entire Hebrew canon. It is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 117 in full. Despite its brevity — two verses — it is regarded as a complete and independent theological statement in the Beta Israel tradition.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 117. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Psalm 117 is two verses that contain the entire scope of covenant theology. Verse 1: all nations — all peoples — are called to praise YHWH. The universal scope of the covenant established with Avraham in Bereshit 12:3 is declared in the simplest possible form. Verse 2: the ground for universal praise is YHWH's steadfast love toward his covenant people and his enduring faithfulness. The nations praise YHWH not for what he has done for them directly but for what his covenant faithfulness to Yisra'EL reveals about his character. The particular covenant becomes the revelation of the universal Creator.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 118 Give Thanks to YHWH — The Stone the Builders Rejected — This Is the Day YHWH Has Made

1 Give thanks to YHWH, for he is good — for his steadfast love endures forever.

2 Let Yisra'EL say, His steadfast love endures forever.

3 Let the house of Aharon say, His steadfast love endures forever.

4 Let those who fear YHWH say, His steadfast love endures forever.

5 Out of my distress I called on YHWH — YHWH answered me and set me free.

6 YHWH is on my side — I will not fear. What can man do to me?

7 YHWH is on my side as my helper — I shall look in triumph on those who hate me.

8 It is better to take refuge in YHWH than to trust in man.

9 It is better to take refuge in YHWH than to trust in princes.

10 All nations surrounded me — in the name of YHWH I cut them off.

11 They surrounded me, surrounded me on every side — in the name of YHWH I cut them off.

12 They surrounded me like bees — they went out like a fire among thorns — in the name of YHWH I cut them off.

13 I was pushed hard, so that I was falling, but YHWH helped me.

14 YHWH is my strength and my song — he has become my salvation.

15 Glad songs of salvation are in the tents of the righteous: the right hand of YHWH does valiantly,

16 The right hand of YHWH exalts — the right hand of YHWH does valiantly.

17 I shall not die, but I shall live, and recount the deeds of YHWH.

18 YHWH has disciplined me severely, but he has not given me over to death.

19 Open to me the gates of righteousness, that I may enter through them and give thanks to YHWH.

20 This is the gate of YHWH — the righteous shall enter through it.

21 I thank you that you have answered me and have become my salvation.

22 The stone that the builders rejected has become the cornerstone.

23 This is YHWH's doing — it is marvelous in our eyes.

24 This is the day that YHWH has made — let us rejoice and be glad in it.

25 Save us, we pray, O YHWH — O YHWH, we pray, give us success.

26 Blessed is he who comes in the name of YHWH — we bless you from the house of YHWH.

27 YHWH is Creator, and he has made his light to shine upon us. Bind the festal sacrifice with cords, up to the horns of the altar.

28 You are my Creator, and I will give thanks to you — you are my Creator — I will extol you.

29 Give thanks to YHWH, for he is good — for his steadfast love endures forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 118 closes the Egyptian Hallel — Psalms 113-118 — and is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm opens and closes with the covenant refrain — his steadfast love endures forever — and contains verse 22 — the stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone — one of the most cited verses in the Second Temple period literature. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 118 in full. Verse 24 — this is the day that YHWH has made — let us rejoice and be glad in it — is central to the Beta Israel festival liturgy, sung at Pesach and Sukkot.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 118. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 22 — the stone that the builders rejected has become the cornerstone. This is one of the most theologically rich verses in the entire Tehillim. The rejected stone — the one evaluated as unfit by those with authority to judge — becomes the cornerstone on which the entire structure rests. YHWH reverses the builders' verdict. What they cast aside becomes the foundation. Verse 24 — this is the day that YHWH has made — let us rejoice and be glad in it. The day of YHWH's making is the day of covenant fulfillment — when the rejected stone is vindicated and the gates of righteousness are opened. Every day of covenant faithfulness is YHWH's day.

END OF PART SIX — PSALMS 107 THROUGH 118
